subject: english
topic_title: Noun Phrases, Peptic Ulcer, and Health Vocabulary
learning_objectives: Apply Noun Phrases correctly in sentences.
Summarize the key ideas in Peptic Ulcer.
Use vocabulary related to Health correctly in context.

sections.heading: Structure: Noun Phrases
sections.content: A noun phrase is a phrase that has the noun as its headword and which performs any of the six functions of a noun – subject, object, complement, etc. Examples: The fat woman with a tiny voice took ill shortly after the exercise. The new English teacher called John a lazy student. She is a woman of virtue. Other Types of Noun phrases: Gerundial Phrase (introduced by a gerundial e.g. The government has banned smoking in public); Infinitive Phrase (identified by an infinitive e.g. It is an offence to smoke in public). Functions of Noun Phrases: Subject of the verb, Object of the verb, Noun phrase as subject complement, Noun phrase as object complement, Noun phrase as complement of preposition, Noun in apposition.

sections.heading: Comprehension: Peptic Ulcer
sections.content: This passage deals with peptic ulcer, which happens when parts of the stomach lining the duodenum are attacked by protein – digesting enzymes, pepsin and hydrochloric acid. The acid of these enzymes give rise to internal bleeding and pain which can only be relieved by taking food in small quantities at frequent intervals.

sections.heading: Vocabulary Development: Words Associated with Health
sections.content: Contract – To get a disease or to be infected. A syndrome – A pattern of illness that go together. Chronic – Long lasting disease or illness. Convalescence – A resting period after recovery from an illness. Transfusion (Blood) – This is giving a person blood that has been supplied by someone else. Viruses – Tiny organisms, smaller than bacteria which cause disease. Contagious – Spread by having contact with a patient suffering it (disease). Coma – State of unconsciousness. Anesthesia – This is given when an operation is about to be carried out. Intravenous – Given through the vein. Debilitating – Weakening. Relapse – Fall back into the illness after recovery.
